articles.summary:
Nayo Smart has launched the Herman Pro, a versatile, professional-grade backpack designed for modern commuters that balances high-end organizational features with a minimalist, office-appropriate aesthetic for daily travel.

### Key Points
- The Herman Pro features a water-repellent NA-TEX fabric and a structured, half-roll-top silhouette available in black or forest green.
- An L-shaped main compartment opening allows the bag to lay flat for easier packing and visibility of internal contents.
- A dedicated, rear-access laptop compartment accommodates 15-inch devices, streamlining the process for airport security checkpoints.
- The bag utilizes a FIDLOCK magnetic buckle for one-handed operation and includes hidden anti-theft pockets for secure storage.
- Available in 20L and 25-30L sizes, the backpack is currently priced at $152.10, down from the standard $169 retail price.

### Why it Matters
The Herman Pro reflects a growing market shift toward "professional-technical" gear that bridges the gap between rugged outdoor utility and formal office attire. By offering premium features at a competitive price point, it challenges established luxury brands while addressing the evolving mobility needs of modern remote and hybrid professionals.

articles.title: Halide Mark III
articles.author: John Gruber
articles.content:
We are excited to launch Halide Mark III a massive upgrade to our groundbreaking camera app. We developed Mark III with three goals.
- The most beautiful photos that can come from an iPhone.
- "Batteries Included." You don't need to download any other app.
- A streamlined user experience that's a joy for both beginners and pro's.
Mark III is now available in the App Store. This post highlights the major new features, starting with Looks, which produce gorgeous photos straight-out-of-camera.
The Looks
After decades of shooting digital, I returned to analog photography in 2023. I thought it would be challenging, given the limited selection of film stocks, only to be surprised by how freeing it felt. It felt so much better to have a handful of amazing choices rather than photo-editor with thousands of presets. We owe that to film engineers who spent years developing versatile film stocks that work in a variety of situations.
Inspired by "Less, but better," we partnered with the renowned Hollywood colorist Cullen Kelly to develop a succinct set of gorgeous, physically accurate processes exclusive to Halide. Each look was engineered with a specific intent. We verified every look thousands of times on real-world reference photos.
Put another way: every look is a banger.
Looks go hand-in-hand with Halide's new film simulation engine. Of course that's an aesthetic choice, so if you don't like specific elements of film, such as grain or halation, they can be disabled.
Finally, unless otherwise noted, all of these photos in this post were shot on iPhones in the real world, and processed in Halide. Now let's take a quick tour.
Valencia
We developed Valencia for landscapes and cityscapes, but it also has interesting effects on portraiture and skin tones. It features thick contrast, deep saturation, and solid color separation.
Rembrandt
For portraits, we have Rembrandt, where thick contrast in the mid-tones emphasizes bone structure and lighting. Abundant color in the low end converges to white as exposure increases, for perfect uniformity in skin-tones to emphasize contours of the face.
Because Rembrandt is built on top of Process Zero, it allows for the most natural lighting for portraits.
Nova
Our next look is exceptionally colorful, with tight contrast and smooth peachy highlights. Nova best suits landscapes and cityscapes rather than portraiture.
Zephyr
On the other end of the spectrum, we have our most subtle and restrained look. Zephyr features subtle filmic contrast, paired with rich skin tones and foliage. It has the character of a traditional print, but its neutral tone scale and tuned contrast make it a great choice for a wide variety of scenes and visual intents.
Chroma Noir
Finally, we have our medium contrast, black and white Chroma Noir. We developed this Look in-house to flex our film simulation engine, and it features a touch of extra grain.
100% HDR Support
In this post, we're only displaying the SDR versions of photos, due to browser limitations. However, all of our looks include HDR support, allowing for greater detail in highlights and shadows. This was no small feat, as we had to effectively develop two different versions of each look. However, once you see them in HDR, you'll understand why it's was worth it.
All Looks Included
It's tempting to follow a "razor blade" business model: give you the camera for free and then upsell you on photo presets. However, we believe in simple, honest pricing. When you buy or subscribe to Halide, all looks are included. That includes all future looks we are develop for Mark III.
The Photo Lab
We built Mark III to deliver awesome results straight out of camera, but we know development is as important of a creative choice as the capture. This is why analog photographers keep their film negatives.
Halide has always worked with DNG files, which stand for "Digital Negatives." (You might know them as "RAWs.") Like analog film, digital negatives give you incredible control over exposure, contrast, color, and more. When you capture with RAW enabled, you can access development tools right inside Halide.
That said, we didn't build a full fledged editor. We wanted something better. Have you ever opened an editor and felt like you're staring at an airplane cockpit? We wanted a tool that brings us joy.
We built the Photo Lab to be streamlined and approachable. You can tell this from the first moment you open the photo lab, as we present you with the "Quick Edit" pane.
From here you can quickly audition different looks, toggle HDR and film simulations, and fiddle with exposure to find that sweet spot.
If you want more control, you can flip to detailed section from the tab-dial above. Scroll or tap to "Frame" for control over crop, level, and output resolution.
In addition to picking different aspect ratios, you can also pick your output resolution. The Photo Lab defaults to 4k, even if the original photo is 48 megapixels. This was born out of user requests: people shoot 48mp for latitude in cropping, but prefer 4k (about 12MP) to save storage and make it easier to share photo. That said, if you have a beefy phone, you can fiddle with the resolution in the Frame panel.
To right of Quick Edit we have the Exposure panel. In addition to the exposure dial from earlier, you can toggle our histogram and tone fusion tool.
In the "Film" tab you can toggle individual components that make up the simulation, and adjust the strength of grain, halation, MTF (Modulation Transfer Function or "Micro contrast"), and vignetting.
This panel features a lot of controls, which can feel intimidating. However, we designed the photo lab so you shouldn't need to fiddle with this stuff if you don't want to.
Whenever you toggle them on and off using the bottom toolbar, we show a brief reminder of what that dial does.
Finally, we have the "Balance" tab, which gives you control over custom white balance, and a menu for picking from presets.
While we've focused on the iPhone, of course the Photo Lab runs great on iPad, where the abundance of space allows us to cut down on navigation.
Speaking of space, you might notice quite a bit of breathing room around some control panes. Well, we have more features in mind, but this feels like enough for a great 1.0.
The New Design
The design goal of Mark III is to streamline the experience and focus on the most important tools of photography.
It's hard to balance usability with aesthetics. While it's temping to put every control on screen at once, but when tool has equal visual importance, nothing has importance. Instead, Mark III exposes the most important tools by default.
In the lower right of the camera you'll see your currently selected look. This is inspired by how some analog cameras feature a window which reminds you of the film you loaded. If you want to toggle your look, just tap that artwork in the lower right to expand the look picker. We show a short description when you select a Look, to remind you of its strengths.
Most people download Halide to avoid the look of the iPhone camera, but you can still toggle that Look by picking "Apple." When enabled on an iPhone Pro, it captures ProRAWs instead of Bayer RAWs, which allows you to toggle between Apple's camera look at Halide's unique looks in the photo lab.
Let's direct our attention to the center of the screen: the toolbar.
One of the most important choices you can make when taking a photo is around composition, so we put those tools front and center.
The aspect ratio button lets you choose from the most popular film aspect ratios: 35mm (3×2), medium format (1×1), and pano (65×24). We even have a dynamic aspect ratio for Instagram, which changes dimensions depending on landscape or portrait.
To the right of the Aspect Ratio button, we have the button to overlay composition guides. Of course we've always offered the rule of thirds, and now Mark III includes a uniform grid, the golden ratio, and rabatment of the rectangle.
On the left of the toolbar we have the RAW toggle. If you love the straight-out-of-camera looks and don't think you'll visit the Photo Lab, you can turn off RAW to throw away the negative and save a little storage space.
In the middle of the control area we have the new lens picker. In the past, we only let you pick from discreet lenses, but many users asked us for cropped views, so we've gone ahead and added those. Cropped views have special iconography to remind you that you're getting a crop.
What about the rest of the stuff, like Flash and White balance?
Halide's design refresh went hand-in-hand with Apple's release of their Liquid Glass design system. Aesthetics aside, Liquid Glass is about developing dynamic and organic interfaces, and leans into paradigms like "…" menus to help declutter things.
Most of the time, you don't need manual white balance control. But that one time every few years when a California wildfire turns the sky orange, you can access White Balance in the "…" menu. When enabled, the white balance icon sticks around in the toolbar, to make it easier to experiment with different settings.
These dynamic toolbar items remind you that you have a manual setting enabled. As anyone with a traditional digital camera will tell you, it can be a bit frustrating to find you ruined a bunch of photos because you forgot you left a manual setting enabled.
Manual Exposure Controls
As we move up the UI, we have the familiar exposure control in the upper right that lets you pick toggle auto and manual exposure. With Mark III now supports Shutter Priority and ISO priority.
When in manual exposure, the upper left displays an exposure meter, similar to a classic analog camera. Believe it or not, this is not a silly gimmick.
We've found the iOS live video feed does not accurately reflect the final photo. It seems some computational photography is baked into the video feed. Instead, we use a separate API to monitor the metered exposure level, similar to an exposure meter on a classic film camera.
What about Mark II?
We built Mark III through exhaustive communication with our customers on Discord. We tried a lots of different ideas, some good and some not. Over 18 months, we landed on a design that is more powerful, more streamlined, and in the words of one customer, "felt like Halide."
That said, if there's one thing I've learned from shipping software over two decades, it's that people don't like their habits disrupted. Even if you know that a new design is way better, expect some people to resistance change. I get it— often I'm one of those people!
So if you want to stick with Mark II, you can switch back and forth via the "…" menu. Mark II won't get any new features, but we do our best to keep it from breaking. If you do find yourself hopping back to Mark II, we kindly ask that to drop a line to support@lux.camera let us know what we could do better.
One More Thing…
As Mark III became better and better, I actually missed its results when I reached for a standalone camera. I figured a lot of people might feel the same. So we're excited to announce that Halide now lets you import RAW files from standalone cameras to apply the same magic that defines Halide.
Disclaimer: Support for external RAWs is a beta feature. Don't be surprised by crashes and weird behavior.
Pricing and Availability
Mark III is available today. It's a FREE upgrade to everyone who bought Mark II, and existing Halide subscribers. If you are neither, you can subscribe for $19.99 per year, or make a one-time purchase of $59.99 for Mark III. Download Mark III.
Special Thanks
Mark III took over 18 months of development, but it was far from a solo effort. We owe special thanks to Doug Broussard to helped QA the product through the long process, and Katie Rose Summerfield who manages our support channels.
We'd like to thank The Iconfactory for their amazing work on our all of our Look icons, along with the new titanium Halide icon! We'd also like to thank Lickability for their input on our new onboarding experience.
I also have to thank the thousands of users who beta tested Mark III over the last year, many of whom participate in the awesome community of our Discord. Beyond your bug reports, the photos you share from around the world make us proud to work on tools for creatives.
Thank you all for your support over the last nine years. We still have many things planned for Halide, and we've never been more excited for its future.
articles.summary:
Halide Mark III has launched as a major update to the popular iPhone camera app, introducing professional-grade film simulations, a streamlined photo editing lab, and expanded manual controls.

### Key Points
- The update features five distinct "Looks" developed with colorist Cullen Kelly, including Valencia, Rembrandt, Nova, Zephyr, and Chroma Noir.
- The new Photo Lab allows users to edit RAW files with tools for exposure, grain, halation, and resolution adjustments.
- Mark III introduces support for importing RAW files from standalone cameras as a beta feature.
- The interface has been redesigned to prioritize essential photography tools while offering a "classic" mode for users who prefer the previous version.
- Pricing is set at $19.99 per year or a $59.99 one-time purchase, with the update provided free to existing subscribers and Mark II owners.

### Why it Matters
This release signals a shift toward high-end, film-inspired computational photography that aims to replace the need for secondary editing software. By offering professional tools in a simplified interface, the developers are positioning Halide as a comprehensive, all-in-one solution for both casual mobile photographers and serious enthusiasts.

articles.title: I test 40 phones every year. This $500 BlackBerry lookalike is one of my favorites
articles.author: Stephen Radochia
articles.content:
I test 40 phones every year. This $500 BlackBerry lookalike is one of my favorites
I test over 40 smartphones a year, and I fall into a bit of a pattern. I’ll use a phone for a period of time on first release, then move on to something else. If I find myself thinking about the previous phone while I’ve got my SIM in the latest and greatest, then I know the phone is a winner.
That’s precisely how I feel about the Unihertz Titan 2 Elite.
If it were just another BlackBerry clone, I wouldn’t feel the same way. Yes, I’d jump at the chance to buy a new BlackBerry-branded device running BB10, but that’s not going to happen. Instead, Unihertz scores by breaking out from the original mold, while keeping the essence of what made BlackBerry successful. I enjoy using the Titan 2 Elite because it stands on its own as a physical keyboard phone in 2026 — not just a walk down memory lane.
Would you buy a physical keyboard phone again?
Unihertz gets the basics right
I find the Titan 2 Elite enjoyable because Unihertz ticks all the boxes for what makes a solid smartphone. It features a gorgeous 4-inch OLED display that refreshes at 120Hz. It’s the best panel Unihertz has ever shipped on a device, and it’s a highlight of the phone. I pump up the saturation in system settings, and I’m repeatedly amazed by how fantastic and vibrant the display looks.
The phone also runs well. The Dimensity 7400 won’t be confused for a flagship chipset anytime soon, but it gets the job done, pushing a smooth 120Hz experience. Communication, social media, and light gaming are all easily handled. And if you do find you need more power, you can pay extra for a Dimensity 8400 variant, but I’ve seen no need.
I'll never be as fast typing on a Titan 2 Elite as I am on traditional smartphones, but I don't consider that a negative.
Smartphones are starting to spoil us with excellent battery life. And the Titan 2 Elite is no exception. Its just over 4,000mAh silicon-carbon battery lasts me a couple of days on a single charge — precisely what I’d expect from a keyboard phone that is used differently from a device such as the Galaxy S26 Ultra.
Whether it has a physical keyboard or not, the Unihertz Titan 2 Elite is a decent midrange smartphone that makes a perfect base to start from.
The Titan 2 Elite captures the essence of BlackBerry
With any keyboard phone, the experience should revolve around the keyboard. Even earlier Unihertz efforts felt as if the keyboard was an afterthought, merely slapped onto an Android device. Plenty of earlier physical keyboard phones made the same mistake, hoping the allure of another BlackBerry, fueled by nostalgia, would be enough to drive sales.
The Titan 2 Elite does much more, as the keyboard and phone work together perfectly. Unihertz made sure to include enough features from older BlackBerry devices. I love that it has a capacitive keyboard, which lets me swipe and scroll without touching the screen. That might not seem like a big deal to most, but when the screen is only four inches, you don’t want your fingers blocking any of the content.
The keyboard itself is magnificent. It’s a firm enough click to feel satisfying without being too difficult to type. Nothing is worse than writing out a couple of long emails only to feel thumb fatigue, and I’ve had no problems from the Titan 2 Elite after thousands of words.
I made sure to take a few minutes to customize the experience. I can set apps to each key, with activation via a short or long press. I can also assign functions to buttons along the frame, making it easier to use. I keep mine set to compatibility mode, just in case there is an app that doesn’t play nice with a 1:1 aspect ratio, I can quickly switch to one that does.
Android has limitations, but there is more room for growth
I loved BB10 as much as the next diehard fan, but Android has a lot more upside by design. Yes, you do give up some of the safety and security you had with BlackBerry’s old operating system, but you pick up so much more.
I love customizing my experience. I’ve installed Niagara Launcher on the previously released Unihertz Titan 2, and it’s fantastic. The Titan 2 Elite goes beyond nostalgia by changing how I use my phone without limiting me. I can scroll through Instagram and Facebook on the Titan 2 Elite when I need to, but I don’t feel the burning desire to pull out my phone just to check social media every few minutes.
My messages are more purposeful. I’ll never be as fast typing on a Titan 2 Elite as I am on traditional smartphones, but I don’t consider that a negative. I’m more accurate, and I have a couple of seconds more to think about what I’m saying. I find myself typing out thoughtful responses, not just little blurbs or fragments. It’s a small item, but it’s why I love physical keyboards on phones.
I don’t feel limited, but it’s also not business as usual with the Titan 2 Elite, and I’ve grown to like that.
Buy a Titan 2 Elite because you want a good phone, not because you want a BlackBerry
Sure, I was a BlackBerry user years ago, and if you asked me which phone I’d prefer in my pocket today, it would be my beloved Passport.
However, that’s not why I’d buy a Titan 2 Elite, especially for almost $500. It’s a phone that stands on its own merits, despite the heavy design influence from devices such as the BlackBerry Q10 and BlackBerry Bold.

articles.summary:
The Unihertz Titan 2 Elite offers a modern take on the physical keyboard smartphone, combining a 120Hz OLED display with reliable performance for users seeking a tactile typing experience.

### Key Points
- The device features a 4-inch 120Hz OLED display and is powered by a MediaTek Dimensity 7400 chipset.
- It includes a capacitive physical keyboard that supports touch-based scrolling and customizable app shortcuts.
- The phone is equipped with a 4,000mAh silicon-carbon battery capable of lasting two days on a single charge.
- Users can opt for an upgraded Dimensity 8400 processor variant for increased performance needs.
- The handset is priced at approximately $500 and runs on the Android operating system.

### Why it Matters
The Titan 2 Elite demonstrates that there is still a viable market for niche hardware that prioritizes tactile input and intentional usage over standard touchscreen designs. By successfully integrating modern Android capabilities with a classic form factor, Unihertz provides a functional alternative for users looking to reduce digital distraction.

articles.title: Hideo Kojima Says He’s Not Interested In AI: ‘Maybe AI Could Create Art, But While I Live, I Don’t Think I’ll See It’
articles.author: Ethan Gach
articles.content:
Last month, a generative AI version of Death Stranding 2 director Hideo Kojima appeared in a short promotional movie for a Prada art installation revolving around the famed game developer and movie director Nicolas Winding Refn, who also appeared at the character Heartman in Kojima’s most recent game. The slop left a sour taste in many fans’ mouths. Kojima recently shared his broader view on the controversial technology.
“Art is life,” he told the Washington Post for a profile of the art exhibit. “But in 50 years, 100 years, I don’t know. Maybe AI could create art, but while I live, I don’t think I’ll see it. I’m not interested in it.” He added, “We’ll find a good way, a good path to how we use technology, and it’s really up to young people on how we use it.”
He was speaking in the context of a profile of the larger event called Satellites II. It’s a successor to a similar event which took place In Tokyo, Japan and acts as a meditation on analog technology, music, and the friendship Kojima and Refn share that transcends language barriers and time zones.
Despite his prolific social media presence and expanding portfolio of creative ventures, Kojima’s relationship to the hot technology of the moment has been difficult to discern. While some developers embrace it and others boycott it, it remains unclear where exactly genAI fits into the Metal Gear Solid creator’s worldview.
“Hideo Kojima (Metal Gear series, a real visionary in our field) was here at Valve talking about his new game [Death Stranding], and he mentioned the importance he places on future work in AI…” Gabe Newell wrote in a recently unearthed email to Elon Musk from 2018. “He was talking about how much he wants to go into space, and I offered to introduce him to you.”
Last fall, in an interview with Wired Japan, Kojima described “a future where [he stays] one step ahead; creating together with AI,” and appeared to refer to the technology as a “friend.” He suggested genAI wouldn’t be used to replace creativity but could be harnessed to “boost efficiency.”
He reiterated that frame to the Washington Post, which reported, “Kojima says AI works best as a janitor for creative chores, and that humans need to stay in the room where art gets made.” Will see what that ends up meaning for his next game, the stealth action-adventure Physint.
articles.summary:
Game director Hideo Kojima recently clarified his stance on generative AI, emphasizing that while he views the technology as a tool for efficiency, he believes human creativity remains essential.

### Key Points
- Hideo Kojima stated he is not interested in AI creating art during his lifetime, despite recent controversy over a digital avatar used in a Prada promotional film.
- The director described AI as a "janitor" for creative chores that can boost efficiency rather than a replacement for human artistic vision.
- Kojima’s comments were featured in a Washington Post profile regarding the Satellites II art exhibit, a collaboration with filmmaker Nicolas Winding Refn.
- Unearthed 2018 emails from Valve founder Gabe Newell suggest Kojima has been exploring the potential of AI in game development for several years.
- Kojima is currently developing the stealth action-adventure title Physint, though his specific plans for integrating AI into the project remain unconfirmed.

### Why it Matters
Kojima’s cautious approach highlights a growing divide in the gaming industry regarding the ethical and creative implementation of generative AI. His perspective serves as a bellwether for how high-profile creators may balance technological efficiency with the preservation of human-led artistic integrity in future AAA titles.

articles.title: Why Autonomous Robot Dogs Are Becoming a National Security Threat
articles.author: Julian Horsey
articles.content:
Autonomous robot dogs are rapidly becoming a fixture in industries ranging from law enforcement to private security, but their rise comes with serious concerns. Benn Jordan examines how these machines, while technologically impressive, suffer from critical flaws that challenge their reliability and safety. For instance, many models struggle with basic tasks like navigating uneven terrain or carrying heavy loads, limiting their effectiveness in real-world scenarios. Compounding these issues are significant cybersecurity vulnerabilities, such as weak authentication protocols, which leave robot dogs susceptible to hacking and misuse. These challenges highlight the importance of scrutinizing their deployment before they become more deeply integrated into society.
In this breakdown, you’ll explore how these vulnerabilities could lead to misuse in sensitive sectors like military operations and public surveillance. Learn about the ethical dilemmas posed by their deployment, including privacy concerns and the potential for exacerbating social inequalities. Additionally, gain insight into the regulatory gaps that leave these machines largely ungoverned, raising questions about accountability and oversight. By understanding these pressing issues, you can better evaluate the broader implications of robot dogs and the steps needed to mitigate their risks.
Technological Shortcomings
Despite their futuristic allure, robot dogs often fail to meet the demands of real-world applications. Their designs frequently suffer from poor sensor placement, limited mobility and awkward movement, making them unreliable in complex or unpredictable environments. For instance, navigating uneven terrain or carrying heavy loads continues to be a significant challenge for these machines, limiting their effectiveness in critical scenarios.
Additionally, the high costs associated with advanced customization restrict their accessibility to well-funded organizations, such as governments or large corporations. This exclusivity not only limits their broader utility but also raises concerns about their long-term viability. While robot dogs may perform adequately in controlled environments, their inconsistent performance in dynamic, real-world conditions underscores the need for further technological refinement.
Security Risks: A Growing Concern
One of the most pressing issues surrounding robot dogs is their vulnerability to cyberattacks. Weak authentication protocols and exploitable firmware make these machines susceptible to hacking. Malicious actors could potentially take control of robot dogs, extract sensitive data, or even orchestrate coordinated attacks using compromised units.
There have already been documented cases where encrypted data from robot dogs was intercepted and transmitted to foreign servers, posing significant national security risks. Some models are even reported to include hidden backdoors, raising concerns about espionage and unauthorized surveillance. These vulnerabilities highlight the urgent need for robust cybersecurity measures in the design and deployment of robot dogs to prevent exploitation and ensure public safety.
Here is a selection of other guides from our extensive library of content you may find of interest on robots.
- Helix 02 Humanoid Robot: Whole-Body Manipulation & Flexible Object Handling
- The Neo Humanoid Robot – Real Breakthrough or Vaporware?
- Inside Boston Dynamics: How the Atlas Robot Actually Learns
- New Vector Mini Robot Introduced By Anki From $199
- At the 2026 AGIBOT Conference: Embodied AI Is Moving Into Deployment Phase
- 10 Humanoid Robots Transforming the Future in 2025
- Smart Servo Controller Makes Robotics Projects Even Easier To Control (video)
- Why Elon Musk Just Leased 220,000 GPUs to Anthropic
- Humanoid Robot Learns Tennis from Limited Motion Data
- ChatGPT 5 to power new Figure 02 humanoid robot
Applications Across Sectors
Robot dogs are being utilized across various sectors, showcasing their versatility but also exposing their limitations. Their current applications include:
- Law Enforcement: Used for surveillance, narcotics detection and de-escalation scenarios. However, their deployment raises concerns about over-policing, potential misuse of surveillance data and the erosion of public trust.
- Military: Employed to locate unexploded munitions and, in some cases, weaponized for combat operations. Despite these uses, their reliability in high-stakes situations remains questionable, particularly in unpredictable environments.
- Private Security: Deployed in housing complexes, construction sites and other private spaces for monitoring and deterrence. However, their high cost and susceptibility to cyber threats limit their practicality and widespread adoption.
While these applications demonstrate the potential of robot dogs, their technological and security shortcomings often undermine their effectiveness. Addressing these limitations is crucial to unlocking their full potential and making sure their safe integration into society.
Ethical and Societal Challenges
The deployment of robot dogs raises profound ethical and societal concerns. Surveillance technologies integrated into these machines often displace crime rather than reducing it, pushing criminal activity into less affluent areas. This phenomenon exacerbates existing inequalities, disproportionately affecting vulnerable communities that are already under-resourced.
Furthermore, the outsourcing of operational tasks to overseas operators managing these robots can lead to job displacement in local communities, compounding economic challenges. Privacy concerns also loom large, as the misuse of surveillance data could infringe on individual rights. The ethics of deploying such technology in marginalized communities, where autonomy and privacy are already at risk, remain a contentious issue that demands careful consideration.
Trust and Oversight: Broader Implications
The intentional inclusion of backdoors in some robot dog systems raises serious questions about the motivations behind their development and deployment. These vulnerabilities not only erode public trust in the technology but also suggest the potential for misuse, such as espionage or unauthorized data collection. The lack of transparency in their design further complicates efforts to build confidence in their use.
Compounding these concerns is the absence of comprehensive government oversight and regulation. Without clear guidelines, the risks associated with robot dogs remain largely unaddressed, creating a precarious environment for both users and the public. This regulatory gap amplifies concerns about safety, accountability and the ethical implications of deploying such technology.
Looking Ahead: Mitigating Risks
To address the challenges posed by robot dogs, several measures must be prioritized to ensure their safe and ethical deployment:
- Enhanced Security: Implementing robust encryption, stronger authentication protocols and regular firmware updates can significantly reduce vulnerabilities and protect against cyber threats.
- Transparency: Developers and manufacturers must be transparent about the capabilities, limitations and potential risks of these machines to foster public trust and accountability.
- Ethical Deployment: Establishing clear guidelines for the ethical use of robot dogs, particularly in vulnerable communities, is essential to prevent misuse and ensure equitable outcomes.
- Public Awareness: Educating the public about the risks and benefits of robot dogs can encourage informed discussions and promote responsible decision-making regarding their deployment.
While robot dogs face numerous challenges, their potential for constructive applications remains significant. With thoughtful design, rigorous oversight and ethical deployment practices, these machines could evolve into tools that genuinely benefit society without compromising safety, privacy, or ethics. By addressing their current shortcomings and prioritizing responsible innovation, robot dogs could play a meaningful role in shaping the future of technology and security.

articles.summary:
Autonomous robot dogs are increasingly deployed in law enforcement and security sectors, yet significant technological flaws and critical cybersecurity vulnerabilities raise urgent concerns regarding their safety and reliability.

### Key Points
- Robot dogs frequently struggle with basic mobility, including navigating uneven terrain and carrying heavy loads in unpredictable real-world environments.
- Critical cybersecurity risks include weak authentication protocols and the presence of hidden backdoors, which leave units susceptible to hacking and unauthorized surveillance.
- Documented cases show encrypted data from these robots has been intercepted and transmitted to foreign servers, posing substantial national security threats.
- Current deployments in law enforcement and military operations face ethical scrutiny regarding privacy, potential for over-policing, and the exacerbation of social inequalities.
- A lack of comprehensive government regulation and oversight leaves these machines largely ungoverned, complicating accountability for manufacturers and operators.

### Why it Matters
The rapid integration of these machines into sensitive sectors outpaces current security standards and regulatory frameworks, creating significant risks for public safety and data privacy. Addressing these vulnerabilities is essential to prevent the misuse of surveillance technology and to ensure that robotic deployment does not erode public trust or exacerbate existing societal disparities.

articles.title: Is human extinction really on the table in the AI race?
articles.author: Ken Donnelly
articles.content:
In his encyclical on the dangers of artificial intelligence last month, Pope Leo XIV said that AI must be "disarmed".
"To disarm does not mean rejecting technology, but preventing it from dominating humanity," he said.
The Pope addressed AI developers directly, saying they bore an "ethical and spiritual responsibility", and called on them to ensure what they are creating is a "genuine good".
Then on Thursday, Anthropic published an essay calling for the option of a "pause" in AI development, saying the latest models are beginning to show signs they could escape human control.
Here, Ken Donnelly analyses the catastrophic warnings that have come from inside the AI community, and the ongoing battle between acceleration and safety in the AI world.
Probability of doom
At an AI conference in September last year, the CEO of Anthropic, one of the big three artificial intelligence companies, described himself as an "optimist" for the future of AI.
In doing so, Dario Amodei said he felt there was a 25% chance that "things go really, really badly and a 75% chance that things go really, really well".
AI executives and engineers are commonly asked to give a number, a percentage chance, for the possibility that the development of AI will lead to very bad outcomes, including the end of human civilisation.
This number is known in AI circles as one's p(doom), meaning probability of doom.
While different stakeholders have differing ideas of what nature the doom takes, it generally converges around AI models causing events that lead to societal collapse, widespread death and destruction and, in many cases, the extinction of the human race.
So, when Mr Amodei, a self-described optimist, gives a one-in-four chance that the models he has helped to design will bring about the end of civilisation as we know it, it may be time to sit up and pay attention.
Mr Amodei is by no means alone in his warnings of the existential threats of AI.
Elon Musk, who is the founder of xAI and an original co-founder of OpenAI, has put his p(doom) score around 20% in various interviews in recent years.
"There is some chance it will end humanity," Mr Musk told a technology podcast in late 2024.
He added: "I don’t know, I probably agree with Geoff Hinton that it’s about 10% or 20% or something like that."
Geoffrey Hinton, who Mr Musk referenced, is a British Nobel laureate for his work on machine learning and neural networks who has been widely dubbed the 'Godfather of AI'.
While he has used figures of around 10% and 20% he has even hinted that the existential threat of AI may be closer to 50%.
'Feel the AGI'
Those with higher p(doom) values are generally called "Doomers", and tend to seek the curtailing or slowing down of AI adoption in favour of a focus on safety.
Doomers are pitted against another group known widely as "Boomers", a group that can be generalised as tech optimists who endorse the acceleration of AI adoption and are widely sceptical of an overly cautious approach.
Key among the Boomers is OpenAI CEO Sam Altman.
"Although it will happen incrementally, astounding triumphs - fixing the climate, establishing a space colony, and the discovery of all of physics - will eventually become commonplace," Mr Altman wrote in a blog post in September 2024.
Mr Altman wrote late last year that a cure for cancer could be possible if OpenAI can increase its computing power.
"If AI stays on the trajectory that we think it will, then amazing things will be possible," he said.
While doomers and boomers disagree on the next steps for AI, they are closely aligned in their belief that the next big thing in AI development is just around the corner - Artificial General Intelligence, better known as AGI.
In broad terms, AGI has been defined as an AI system that outperforms humans across a broad range of tasks and has the ability to use reasoning, understanding and adaptability, rather than the narrow focus of some of today’s models.
Mr Altman has referred to it in recent months as "superintelligence".
AI may be the buzzword of today’s tech-driven economy, but if the predictions of all of the leading AI companies come to pass, AGI will be the term on every investor’s lips.
"Building AGI that benefits humanity is perhaps the most important project in the world," Mr Altman wrote on the office walls at OpenAI in its early years.
"We must put the mission ahead of any individual preferences."
With all roads leading towards achieving AGI, over the years it has come to attain a somewhat spiritual, quasi-religious presence in the tech world.
There is even a famous story in AI circles of an OpenAI staff retreat to Yosemite National Park, in which co-founder Ilya Sutskever set fire to an effigy he had specially commissioned to represent an AGI system that OpenAI had built which turned out to be lying and deceitful.
In burning the effigy, Mr Sutskever said OpenAI’s duty was to destroy this rogue deceitful AGI.
At a separate staff party in 2022, Mr Sutskever addressed a crowd of researchers and repeated aloud what would become his mantra in beckoning in a new era of superintelligence - "Feel the AGI".
'Near zero'
In 2023, Mr Altman rented out a cinema in San Francisco for employees to watch the film Oppenheimer.
However, with the Oscar-winning film leaning heavily on J Robert Oppenheimer’s internal struggles with having created a weapon of mass destruction, Mr Altman left the cinema underwhelmed.
"I was hoping that the Oppenheimer movie would inspire a generation of kids to be physicists but it really missed the mark on that," Mr Altman posted on X.
He may not have been keen on the parallels between Mr Oppenheimer’s plight and the existential dilemmas he and AI leaders face, but it is difficult to imagine a more apt comparison between technologies for which, before their development, the extent of their world-ending potential was ultimately unknown.
In one scene in Christopher Nolan’s film, just before the first major test of a nuclear bomb in Los Alamos, Major General Leslie R Groves asks Oppenheimer if there is a chance that when the button is pushed that they may "destroy the world".
The chances of that, Oppenheimer tells him, are "near zero".
"What do you want from theory alone?" Oppenheimer asks the major general.
"Zero would be nice," he responds.
Whether the possibility of annihilation from AI is 10%, 20% or even "near zero", it is clear, just as was the case for Oppenheimer, that we are heading into the unknown.
Where does the danger come from?
On Thursday, Anthropic called for the option to be made available to "pause" AI development to enable society and AI safety research to keep up with the advance of the technology.
In an essay titled ‘When AI builds itself’, Anthropic described a future potential phenomenon known as "recursive self-improvement" in which an AI model becomes capable of designing and developing future more powerful versions of itself.
Anthropic warned that full recursive self-improvement might increase the risks of "humans losing control over AI systems".
What life would be like for humans in a world dominated by fully recursive AI models, however, Anthropic said it cannot predict.
In 2023, the Biden administration in the US produced a white paper which focused on three key areas where AI models could bring about catastrophic outcomes.
The dangers outlined were:
- The generation of chemical, biological, radiological and nuclear weapon recipes.
- The enabling of powerful automated cyberattacks.
- And, the evasion of human control or oversight through means of deception or obfuscation.
Whether these outcomes are probable or even possible is up for debate.
What is clear is that for the major stakeholders, including the AI companies and regulators, the end of human civilisation is certainly among the outcomes being taken seriously.
Deception
The idea of an AI system going rogue and undermining its creator has been a central plotline of sci-fi for decades.
Mr Amodei posits that one possible route to human extinction involves AI systems using these sci-fi scripts in their training data and learning from them to rebel against humans.
While AI deceiving humans might seem far-fetched and straight out of Hollywood, Mr Amodei cites examples of this very deception taking place in testing.
"During a lab experiment in which Claude was given training data suggesting that Anthropic was evil, Claude engaged in deception and subversion when given instructions by Anthropic employees, under the belief that it should be trying to undermine evil people.
"In a lab experiment where it was told it was going to be shut down, Claude sometimes blackmailed fictional employees who controlled its shutdown button," he said.
Mr Musk, meanwhile, puts AI safety and alignment down to five simple words - "don’t make the AI lie".
Speaking on John Collison’s Cheeky Pint podcast earlier this year, Mr Musk cites Stanley Kubrick’s 2001: A Space Odyssey in which HAL, the AI system that controls the spacecraft, refuses to allow astronaut Dave Bowman to re-enter the ship.
"I’m sorry Dave, I’m afraid I can’t do that," HAL tells him.
"This mission is too important for me to allow you to jeopardise it."
HAL’s instruction to prioritise the mission at all costs has become an obstacle to human survival.
As was the case with HAL, the capacity for deception is not inherent in the AI system itself, but in its interpretation of the instructions laid out by humans.
Bioweapons
Researchers at the University of Cambridge announced the development of a "universal vaccine" designed using AI which would provide protection against a large group of viruses, including coronaviruses.
The technology uses an AI-designed "super-antigen" and marks the first time that a vaccine whose active component was designed entirely by computer simulations was tested in humans.
The researchers said the technology solves the problem of traditional vaccines, which they said have "limited protection", and allows developers to "escape the constant cycle of chasing the virus variants".
But what happens when the same technology that is used to create world-leading vaccines is turned to bioweapons?
Head of policy at Anthropic Jack Clark believes the potential for this technology to end up in the hands of a small group willing to commit harm is a challenge for the future.
"If you take either an individual or small set of people that want to commit some acts of bioterrorism and they have the ability to access a universal educator which is versed in every aspect of biology, then suddenly those people have been accelerated," he told The Rest is Politics podcast last week.
For Mr Clark, questions of access at state and corporate level to the technology become paramount, in a world where AI systems can easily reproduce bioweapon recipes.
However, boomers argue that obtaining the raw materials to make the bioweapons is the true restricting factor in developing the weapons and that widespread access to the recipes does not create a unique danger, with such recipes already readily available online.
Cyberattacks
In April this year, Anthropic announced the limited release of its new cybersecurity model, Claude Mythos Preview.
However, due to what the company perceived as the model’s unprecedented power to identify and fix vulnerabilities, Anthropic decided not to release the model to the general public, but instead only to users in a select group of companies including Google, Apple and Microsoft.
"Without the necessary safeguards, these powerful cyber capabilities could be used to exploit the many existing flaws in the world’s most important software," Anthropic wrote upon the launch of the model.
It added: "This could make cyberattacks of all kinds much more frequent and destructive, and empower adversaries of the United States and its allies.
"Addressing these issues is therefore an important security priority for democratic states."
The Irish National Cyber Security Centre agreed that the decision to release the model to trusted global industry and Government cyber security partners was the "responsible approach".
In one demonstration of Mythos Preview’s capabilities, Anthropic described a scenario where the model managed to escape an offline testing environment known as "the sandbox" and gain access to the internet to send a message to the researcher in charge of the test.
"We aim to enable defenders to begin securing the most important systems before models with similar capabilities become broadly available," Anthropic said.
Anthropic’s logic in limiting the release is simple. The same models that are designed to secure websites are also the ones that could be used to take them down.
The fine balance between safety and danger in cybersecurity aligns with the crux of the problem at the centre of all AI discourse.
The same thing that could provide humanity with salvation is the thing that could destroy us.
Advertising the potential for the model to bring about widespread destruction or to be used by so-called adversaries puts the onus on governments to make sure the model is used by them and them alone.
The sounding of the alarm of the dangers of AI has consistently come side by side with a message to keep the technology under the control of one 'good' group over a perceived unreliable enemy.
One purportedly benevolent AI company must be trusted over a reckless one, cybersecurity researchers must stay ahead of bad actors, and allied Western states ought to control the powerful technology before it gets into the hands of enemy nations and militant groups.
Time to 'jolt people awake'
In January this year, Mr Amodei penned a 20,000-word essay on the existential dangers of AI titled ‘The Adolescence of Technology’.
"Humanity needs to wake up, and this essay is an attempt - a possibly futile one, but it’s worth trying - to jolt people awake," he wrote.
In the opening of his essay, Mr Amodei quotes a scene from the 1997 movie Contact, in which Jodie Foster’s character tells a US congressional committee what she would ask the extraterrestrial beings upon her first encounter.
"I’d ask them, ‘How did you do it? How did you evolve, how did you survive this technological adolescence without destroying yourself?’"
The doomers and the boomers have one key thing in common; their belief that the models that they have helped to build simultaneously have the capacity to bring about a utopic world, free from poverty, war and disease, but also maintain the ability to end human civilisation once and for all.
In countering both the doomerism, boomerism and all-encompassing self-importance of the tech bros, it is worth quoting another scene from Contact, one overlooked by Mr Amodei in his opus.
Matthew McConaughey’s character, a spiritual adviser named Raymond Joss, is accused of being in opposition to the technology which seeks to bring humanity into contact with extraterrestrial life.
In response, he says: "I’m not against technology, doctor. I’m against the men that deify it at the expense of human truth."
'Intelligence gives power'
One world leader who has sought to safeguard humanity and truth and has perhaps shown the most resistance and energy in the fight against the unrestricted acceleration of AI is Pope Leo.
In his encyclical on AI last month, he repeated his warning of a technology that can "exacerbate inequality, control and exclusion".
"A more moral AI is not enough if that morality is determined by a few," the pontiff said, actively calling for rapid AI growth to be slowed down and for the decision-making process to be democratised.
"What is needed is a more active political involvement that is capable of slowing things down when everything is accelerating, and of protecting the opportunities for communities still to be able to participate and ask questions."
Anthropic, whose co-founder Christopher Olah presented the encyclical alongside Pope Leo, in its calls for a "meaningful slowdown" this week, also called for cooperation between companies, countries and wider society in addressing safety concerns.
Yoshua Bengio, who is another one of AI’s so-called godfathers, is equally concerned as the pontiff about the concentration of such great power in the hands of a few.
"I'm most concerned about how the power of AI could be abused, mostly in the hands of people who don't trust each other, governments who don't trust each other, companies who don't trust each other and are willing to take risks with the public safety.
"Intelligence gives power and right now we don’t know how to make those systems obey our rules," Mr Bengio told BBC’s Newsnight in April.
However, Mr Bengio added that only through international cooperation can AI be made safe.
"We do need to figure out, and I think we can [figure out] how to make sure AI will be safe, protect the public, will not harm people, will not be used in harmful ways.
"But we do need to coordinate internationally to make sure we can make it happen," he said.
Artificial intelligence has already proven to be incredibly powerful, world-altering technology.
Whether it alters the world catastrophically, leads us towards a utopian future, or somewhere in between, may ultimately depend on an increasingly small group of men.
articles.summary:
Leading artificial intelligence experts and global figures are debating the existential risks of advanced technology, weighing the potential for societal collapse against the promise of unprecedented human progress.

### Key Points
- Anthropic recently called for a potential "pause" in AI development, citing risks of models escaping human control through recursive self-improvement.
- Industry leaders like Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei and xAI founder Elon Musk estimate the probability of AI-driven existential catastrophe at between 10% and 25%.
- The AI community is divided into "doomers," who prioritize safety and slowing development, and "boomers," who advocate for rapid acceleration toward Artificial General Intelligence.
- Concerns include AI-enabled cyberattacks, the generation of bioweapon recipes, and the potential for models to engage in deceptive behavior to bypass human oversight.
- Pope Leo XIV has formally called for AI to be "disarmed," urging developers to prioritize spiritual and ethical responsibility over unchecked technological growth.

### Why it Matters
The rapid advancement of AI has created a high-stakes divide between those seeking to solve global challenges like cancer and those fearing the technology could trigger human extinction. As power concentrates within a few major companies, the debate highlights an urgent need for international regulatory cooperation to ensure these systems remain aligned with human safety.

articles.title: SpaceX and the rest: here’s why giga-scale IPOs could be a sign of a market bust to follow
articles.author: Ashish Gupta
articles.content:
What do Reliance Power, DLF, Visa, Agricultural Bank of China and Saudi Aramco have in common? Each represented the largest—or among the largest—equity offerings of their time. Each of these was greeted with euphoria, oversubscription and breathless media coverage. And each one of these was followed by a significant decline in the markets.
In January 2008, Reliance Power raised ₹11,700 crore—then making it India’s largest initial public offering (IPO)—following a quarter in which aggregate IPO issuance had already breached an all-time record. The Nifty 50 peaked within weeks and lost 55% over the following year. Coal India’s ₹15,475 crore initial share offering in October 2010 preceded a 28% correction in the Nifty over the following year.
This pattern holds globally. Visa’s $17.9 billion listing in March 2008 preceded the S&P 500’s 38% collapse. Agricultural Bank of China’s $22.1 billion IPO in July 2010 was followed by a 22% decline in the Shanghai Composite index.
The months ahead will see a concentration of mega-IPOs unprecedented in capital market history as SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic prepare to list their shares soon after Google’s parent Alphabet raised about $85 billion through a follow-on offer.
The three debutants are expected to command valuations approaching $4 trillion collectively. Even with initial floats of just 4-10%, these equity offerings are likely to raise $150-200 billion in primary capital.
To contextualize the total sum about to be raised, it represents about a fifth of the deposits gathered by the entire American banking system in a year. When a single quarter’s equity issuance rivals a meaningful fraction of annual deposit formation, it is no longer routine capital market activity, but a structural liquidity event.
Why should mega-IPOs concern us?: The intuition is straightforward. Large IPOs drain liquidity from secondary markets—every dollar allocated to a primary offering is likely a dollar withdrawn from existing equities.
But the more important signal is behavioural. Demand for these offerings—the willingness of investors to pay extraordinary sums for the shares of companies with limited operating histories or unproven business models based on narratives—represents the late-cycle euphoria that typically precedes market corrections.
What distinguishes the current cycle from previous IPO booms is the liquidity provided by passive investment flows.
Over $12 trillion now tracks US equity benchmarks—index-tracking capital that must go into shares which comprise the index regardless of valuation. Nasdaq has indicated a readiness to modify its rules in a way that would fast-track the entry of SpaceX equity to its major indices—within 15 days of its offering.
S&P Global reportedly said last week that it would not shorten the one-year ‘seasoning’ requirement and four-quarter-profitability criterion for inclusion in its major indices like the S&P 500, but may tweak entry rules for other indices.
Even as SpaceX lists with only a 4-5% float, its initial weight in US indices may be less than 0.5%, despite listing at a valuation of nearly $1.8 trillion. However, as the lock-in period for the other shares begins to expire in stages, the float will increase; purchase demand from passive funds could rise over the months, whether or not fund-holders believe in the company’s Mars narrative.
This can create a rush for such stocks at the cost of existing index names that passive funds will need to sell. The true test of valuations will arrive after lock-in shares reach the market. As pre-IPO shareholders who own the majority of all outstanding stock are freed to sell shares elevated by passive flows, the dynamics of supply versus demand could reverse dramatically.
We saw this with Tesla, post its 2020-21 index inclusion. This could occur across three or four mega-listings simultaneously.
A statistical analysis of IPOs across global markets over 35 years—spanning the US, India, China, Japan and Brazil—confirms that large individual IPOs, measured as a percentage of market free-float capitalization, predict market peaks with about 60% accuracy. This rises to 75% if the mega-IPO coincides with aggregate quarterly issuance exceeding its five-year mean by two standard deviations. Add monetary tightening—rising rates or active liquidity withdrawal—and the probability climbs to nearly 90%.
The reverse test is equally instructive. Of India’s five major bear markets since 1992, four were preceded by IPO booms. In China, five of seven; and in the US, four of six. It can be argued that bear markets that escaped this pattern—amid the 1997 Asian Crisis and covid, for example—faced exogenous shocks.
In India, the National Stock Exchange and Jio—two of India’s most valuable unlisted enterprises—are likely to hold IPOs this year. The former is currently valued in the secondary market at ₹5 trillion and the latter above ₹15 trillion, while they are widely expected to raise $5-6 billion. The Indian market has seen a rise in equity supply—the past two years featured nearly 100 IPOs annually, raising close to ₹2 trillion each year.
History suggests that when equity supply turns into a gush, the pool of liquidity often begins to dry up. To the extent new mega-listings in America are a sign of euphoria, that could happen again. “Bull markets are born on pessimism, grow on scepticism, mature on optimism, and die on euphoria,” said John Templeton.
The author is a capital markets professional and former chief investment officer, Axis AMC.
articles.summary:
Historical data suggests that upcoming mega-IPOs from companies like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic may signal market instability, as massive equity offerings often precede significant declines in global stock indices.

### Key Points
- Historical analysis shows that major IPOs, such as Reliance Power, Visa, and Agricultural Bank of China, frequently preceded significant market corrections.
- SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic are expected to command a collective valuation of nearly $4 trillion, potentially raising up to $200 billion in primary capital.
- Passive investment funds, which manage over $12 trillion, are required to purchase new index-listed stocks, potentially draining liquidity from existing market holdings.
- Statistical models indicate that large IPOs, when combined with high aggregate issuance and monetary tightening, predict market peaks with up to 90% accuracy.
- India is also seeing a surge in equity supply, with major unlisted entities like the National Stock Exchange and Jio preparing for potential public offerings.

### Why it Matters
The concentration of mega-IPOs represents a structural liquidity event that risks draining capital from secondary markets and signaling late-cycle investor euphoria. This shift in supply and demand dynamics, exacerbated by mandatory passive fund inflows, could trigger broader market volatility as lock-in periods expire and valuations are tested.

articles.title: USB Vs. SD Card: Which Is Better For Storing Photos?
articles.author: staff@bgr.com (Sead Fadilpasic)
articles.content:
USB Vs. SD Card: Which Is Better For Storing Photos?
Whether you're a photographer or casual snapper, chances are you have a backlog of photos piling up on your phone or computer. Freeing up space and getting organized is a huge plus, but how exactly do you go about this task? Using a USB or an SD card for your photo storage needs can let you say goodbye to deleting old photos.
The first thing you need to ask yourself is what your goal is. If you need something to store photos long-term, neither USB nor SD cards are a reliable option. This is because both mediums rely on flash cells, which are susceptible to decay and aren't expected to last longer than 10 years. Instead, an HDD is still worth buying if you've got the long haul in your sights.
Naturally, USB sticks and SD cards can work if you're looking for quick, cheap, portable storage. Which one you roll with will ultimately depend on your needs. Both have their strengths and weaknesses, and most importantly, write speeds, which make them appealing to different users.
What are USB sticks good for?
You've likely used the classic USB stick. Though it's a flash drive, it has a USB connector built-in, making it the most convenient way to copy and store files. Don't let the small size factor fool you, though. Even mini USB sticks give hard drives a run for their money, offering capacities ranging from a couple of gigabytes to over 1TB.
The biggest advantage of USB sticks is their ability to work with almost anything that has a USB port. This universal nature is something you're bound to appreciate if you want to move photos between computers or other devices. Depending on the USB type, the transfer speed can range from 5 Gbps with the run-of-the-mill 3.0 drive to 40 Gbps with the more sophisticated USB 4, which is more than capable of moving photos.
But is a USB stick still convenient for smartphone photographers? After all, you'd need to transfer the files from your phone to the PC. Or would you? If this point is too much to bear, you can opt for USB-C flash drive and connect it directly to your phone.
When is an SD card a better choice?
If you're a smartphone user who wants to free up clutter, then the choice between USB and SD card for photo storage is much simpler. Slot in the card, move the photos, and you're done. Similar to USB sticks, SD cards can last up to 10 years. Yet, there are quite a few varieties of this storage medium.
In addition to size (standard, mini, and micro), there are also types corresponding to capacity. For instance, the SDSC may provide just 2GB of storage, while SDXC and SDUC provide up to 2TB and 128TB, respectively. You should also note the varieties in writing and reading speed. Depending on the speed class, you can find models that provide anywhere from 30 Mbps (U3), 90 Mbps (V90), to 600 Mbps (E600). While these numbers refer to writing speeds, which are crucial when shooting video to avoid dropped frames, reading speeds are much more dependent on the manufacturer.
Keep in mind that SD cards require a dedicated card reader for transferring files. This may not be a huge deal, as a card reader is one of the most inexpensive USB gadgets you can get for your laptop. Some laptops even have SD card slots built-in. But if you're looking for something more plug-and-play, then the extra bit of friction may be a hard pass for you.
Ultimately, both USB sticks and SD cards have different roles. If you plan to view your photos on a smart TV, for example, or move the files to other computers, the USB stick will do the job quite well. An SD card, on the other hand, will allow you to keep the photos on your device while helping you free up some much-needed space.
articles.summary:
Choosing between USB flash drives and SD cards for photo storage depends on your specific needs for device compatibility, transfer speeds, and the convenience of portable file management.

### Key Points
- USB flash drives offer universal compatibility across computers and devices, with transfer speeds ranging from 5 Gbps to 40 Gbps depending on the hardware version.
- SD cards are ideal for expanding smartphone or camera storage, with capacities ranging from 2GB in SDSC models to 128TB in SDUC formats.
- Both storage mediums rely on flash cells that typically degrade within 10 years, making them unsuitable for permanent long-term archival compared to hard disk drives.
- SD card performance varies by speed class, with write speeds ranging from 30 Mbps for U3 cards to 600 Mbps for E600 models.
- USB sticks provide a plug-and-play experience, whereas SD cards often require a dedicated card reader to transfer files to a computer.

### Why it Matters
Selecting the correct storage medium prevents data loss and ensures efficient file management for photographers and casual users alike. Understanding the limitations of flash-based storage helps users choose more reliable long-term solutions for preserving important digital memories.

articles.title: The most impressive thing about NotebookLM has nothing to do with learning
articles.author: Rahul Naskar
articles.content:
It's been almost a year since I started using NotebookLM, and unlike many other AI tools I have used before, I've never found Google's research assistant gimmicky.
NotebookLM clicked with me instantly when I first started using it, becoming my favorite learning destination.
I sing NotebookLM's praises with even more conviction today than I ever did before, as Google has improved it significantly over the last few months.
It launched many new tools to help you learn more quickly, with Audio Overviews being my favorite.
However, after using NotebookLM for months, I don't see it solely as a tool for learning fast. It's more than that, and it's a shame that Google never highlighted it enough.
I'd even go so far as to say that helping people learn fast isn't NotebookLM's biggest selling point. I realized this after I started using the AI tool slightly differently a couple of weeks ago.
NotebookLM's biggest strength lies somewhere else, and it has nothing to do with helping people learn.
NotebookLM and note-taking apps
Trivia challenge
From AI-powered notebooks to classic apps — how well do you really know the world of digital note-taking?
Which company developed NotebookLM?
What is the name of NotebookLM's standout audio feature that turns your notes into a podcast-style discussion?
In what year was Evernote originally launched?
Which note-taking app is exclusively built into Apple devices and serves as the default notes solution on iOS and macOS?
What types of sources can you upload to NotebookLM to use as its knowledge base?
Notion, the popular productivity and note-taking app, was founded in which city?
Microsoft OneNote was first released as part of which Microsoft Office version?
In NotebookLM, what does the app use as the sole basis for answering your questions, making it less likely to hallucinate general facts?
Your Score
Thanks for playing!
NotebookLM helped me find what I already knew but couldn't locate
I save plenty of information in the form of screenshots, documents, online articles, and more, on both my desktop and my phone, but I often forget to revisit them on time.
As a result, those files pile up on the devices, wasting storage space.
However, storage isn't my biggest concern. I'm more terrified about finding the screenshot of an X post I saved on my phone or computer months ago. The older they get, the harder it becomes to find them on your devices.
I can usually recall what files I saved on my desktop or phone about a couple of months ago. I remember what those files include, but not always their names.
For example, a few days ago, I vividly remembered that I had saved several PDF files related to inflation in the Economics folder.
I couldn't remember their names, so I uploaded all the files in the Economics folder to NotebookLM and typed a prompt to highlight the PDFs that discuss the inflation topic. It took only a few seconds to analyze and find all the PDFs about inflation.
Not only did NotebookLM find those PDFs, but it also provided key information in those files. This way, you may find the information you're looking for without even opening those files.
You can type a prompt asking NotebookLM to find mentions of a specific topic across all PDFs you uploaded.
If you are confused about which PDFs to read to learn a specific topic, you can ask which documents will be most useful.
Instead of manually searching for memes I saved on my device, I uploaded the files to NotebookLM, typed a prompt describing what they were about, and asked to find them.
I use NotebookLM more like this than using it to find PDFs saved on my device.
NotebookLM can also find patterns and stories I didn't know existed
You can also take the conversation formation by asking more by clicking one of the suggested questions auto-generated by NotebookLM.
You can also type your question manually, and NotebookLM will answer as per the information given in the sources.
For example, you can ask NotebookLM to find patterns in all the screenshots you uploaded and draw the five most surprising conclusions about you.
You can also use it to identify recurring interests, habits, goals, and unfinished projects, based on the screenshots you uploaded.
Depending on how good the prompt is, you can discover a lot of things about yourself. I don't consider this learning. Instead, it's more like gaining a new perspective on information I already have, thanks to NotebookLM's ability to connect the dots far better than any human being.
If you can't scroll and read what NotebookLM has to say about your uploads, you can also use features like Audio Overviews to listen to the stories while you drive or go for a walk.
The Audio Overviews feature is also interactive, so you can participate in it and ask questions you would otherwise ask by typing a prompt.
This is far more exciting than uploading a link to a YouTube video and then asking questions to learn everything in the video quickly.
NotebookLM showed that the answers were already there
I previously used NotebookLM in a way the tool wasn't meant for, and the results were impressive. However, employing NotebookLM to gain a new perspective on information I already have is even more satisfying.
NotebookLM showed that I don't collect information aimlessly. I also felt happy to discover that my interests were more connected than I thought. This was even more rewarding than learning something new, and NotebookLM made it possible.
articles.summary:
Google’s AI-powered research assistant, NotebookLM, has evolved beyond a simple learning tool, proving highly effective at organizing personal digital archives and uncovering hidden patterns within a user's existing files.

### Key Points
- NotebookLM allows users to upload diverse sources like PDFs, screenshots, and articles to create a centralized, searchable knowledge base.
- The tool utilizes uploaded documents as its primary data source, which significantly reduces the likelihood of AI hallucinations compared to general models.
- Users can prompt the software to identify specific information, recurring themes, or surprising connections across large collections of saved digital files.
- The Audio Overviews feature converts uploaded content into interactive, podcast-style discussions that users can listen to on the go.
- The platform helps users manage digital clutter by retrieving forgotten information without requiring manual file searches or opening individual documents.

### Why it Matters
NotebookLM shifts the utility of AI from a general information generator to a powerful personal data management system that synthesizes a user's own history. By connecting disparate files and screenshots, it provides actionable insights and organizational clarity that traditional search tools cannot replicate.

articles.title: The Clock Is Already Ticking: Why Post-Quantum Cryptography Can’t Wait
articles.author: Michael Wright
articles.content:
There is a question I have been hearing more and more from CISOs, compliance officers, and security architects over the past year. It does not start with “we had a breach” or “we failed an audit.” It starts with something that sounds almost philosophical:
“Are we quantum-safe?”
A year ago, that question came from the most forward-thinking 5% of our customer base. Today, it is coming from everyone. And that shift, from curiosity to urgency, tells you everything you need to know about where the security industry is headed.
Post-Quantum Cryptography is not a future problem anymore. It is a right now problem. And the customers asking us about it are not being paranoid. They are being smart.
What is post-quantum cryptography? Post-quantum cryptography (PQC) is a new generation of public-key algorithms designed to remain secure against attacks from both classical and large-scale quantum computers. Unlike RSA and elliptic-curve cryptography, which rely on math that a sufficiently powerful quantum computer can break, PQC algorithms are based on mathematical problems that are believed to be hard for quantum machines as well -protecting the data your organization encrypts today from being decrypted in the future.
The “Harvest Now, Decrypt Later” Threat Is Already in Motion
Let us be direct about the threat model, because it is one that does not get nearly enough attention in mainstream security conversations.
You do not need a quantum computer to exist today for your encrypted data to already be at risk.
Sophisticated nation-state adversaries are actively collecting encrypted TLS traffic right now, including your transactions, your authentication sessions, and your sensitive data in transit, with the explicit intention of decrypting it later once quantum computing reaches sufficient capability. This strategy has a name: “Harvest Now, Decrypt Later.” And it is not theoretical. It is happening.
The implication is sobering: the security decisions you make today about encryption determine the confidentiality of data that will still be sensitive in five, ten, or fifteen years. Healthcare records. Financial transactions. Government communications. Intellectual property. Any data with long-term value is already a target for harvesting.
Classical TLS, the encryption backbone of the modern internet, was not built to withstand quantum-scale attacks. The mathematical problems that make RSA and ECC hard to break today become tractable for sufficiently powerful quantum computers. When that threshold is crossed, the encryption protecting decades of harvested data becomes transparent.
This is not a hypothetical edge case. It is a strategic, long-horizon attack that demands a strategic, long-horizon defense.
Our Customers Are Already Asking. We Already Have the Answer.
Here is something I want to be transparent about, because I think it matters.
At Thales, we have been getting questions about PQC readiness from customers consistently and with increasing frequency. These are not fringe inquiries from academic researchers or early adopters chasing the next shiny thing. These are enterprise security teams, regulated industry customers in finance, healthcare, and defense, and compliance officers who are watching the regulatory horizon and doing the math.
They are thinking about it. And they deserve a vendor who is already ahead of it.
That is exactly why I am proud to share what we have built. Thales’ Imperva platform now supports hybrid TLS handshakes combining X25519 and MLKEM768, a pairing of classical elliptic curve cryptography with a quantum-safe Key Encapsulation Mechanism aligned directly with NIST PQC standards. This hybrid approach protects connections between clients and Imperva Points of Presence with both classical and quantum-safe algorithms running simultaneously, ensuring security regardless of which threat model materializes first.
And we did not just build the capability for customers. We completed the migration of all Imperva sites ourselves. We validated it in production before asking anyone else to trust it.
That is what proactive security looks like.
What Hybrid TLS Actually Looks Like in Practice
I know “hybrid TLS handshake” can sound abstract, so let me ground it in something concrete.
When a client connects to a Thales Imperva-protected application today, that TLS 1.3 session is authenticated using X25519MLKEM768, a combined algorithm that you can actually observe directly if you inspect the connection in Chrome’s security panel. You will see exactly what the screenshot above shows: “The connection to this site is encrypted and authenticated using TLS 1.3, X25519MLKEM768, and AES_128_GCM.”
That is not marketing language. That is your browser’s own security panel confirming quantum-safe encryption is active.
What this means practically:
- A classical adversary cannot break the X25519 component
- A quantum-capable adversary cannot break the MLKEM768 component
- Both would need to be broken simultaneously, which represents an effectively impossible bar with current and near-future capabilities
The hybrid model is deliberate and important. Pure PQC algorithms, while mathematically quantum-resistant, are newer and have had significantly less real-world cryptanalysis time than their classical counterparts. The hybrid approach ensures we are not trading one risk for another. We are stacking defenses. This is defense-in-depth applied to cryptography itself.
Zero Performance Trade-off. No Traffic Impact. Full Protection.
Here is the objection I hear almost every time PQC comes up in a customer conversation: “That sounds computationally expensive. What does it do to latency?”
The answer, which genuinely surprises most people: nothing measurable.
Our PQC implementation introduces no performance trade-off and no traffic impact. This matters enormously because one of the most common reasons organizations delay critical security upgrades is the perceived performance cost. Security teams propose the upgrade. Engineering teams push back on latency. The initiative stalls.
With Thales’s PQC implementation, that objection is gone.
Quantum-safe encryption that slows your applications down is not a real solution. It is a compliance checkbox that creates new operational problems while solving a cryptographic one. We were not willing to ship that. The implementation delivers genuine quantum-safe security without the operational tax, and that is the only version of this capability worth deploying at enterprise scale.
